content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November
17,
1992
Dana
W.
Reed
Reed
& Davidson
3151 Airway Ave.,
Ste.
M-l
Costa Mesa,
CA
92626
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-580
Dear Mr.
Reed:
You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
!
QTJESTION
Are you required to disclose the pro—rata value of benefits
received at an event as determined by the sponsor of the event,
or
may you calculate and report the fair market value of what you
actually consumed at the event?
CONCLUSION
To determine the amount of benefit provided to you,
you must
include payments made by the sponsor for rental of the facilities,
entertainment,
decorations,
and other costs in addition to
payments for food,
beverages,
and other tangible benefits provided
to you.
However,
if you determine that the amount of benefit you
received is less than the amount attributed to you by the sponsor,
you may calculate the fair market value of the actual benefit you
received.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
PD. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(9t6) 322-5660


Dana
W.
Reed
Page
2
FACTS
You are a member of the Orange County Transportation
Authority Board of Directors and are required to file statements
of economic interests
(Form 730)
pursuant to the Authority’s
conflict of interest code.
You attended a meeting of the board of directors of a
nonprofit,
tax-exempt business league held at a hotel in Orange
County.
The event included tennis and golf,
a cocktail reception,
dinner,
and dancing.
You did not participate in the golf or tennis activities.
You attended the cocktail reception but did not drink,
attended
the dinner,
and listened to some of the music.
You have received notification from the sponsor that,
according to their calculations,
your pro rata share of the cost
of the event was
in excess of the $50 reporting threshold for
gifts.
ANALYSIS
The Act requires public officials to file periodic reports
disclosing their personal financial interests and income,
including gifts.
(Section 87200, et g.)
“Gift” is defined in
Section 82020 to mean:
any payment to the extent that consideration of
equal or greater value is not received.
The Commission has advised that for purposes of determining
the amount of benefit provided to an official at a reception or
other event like the one you attended,
the sponsor must include
payments for room rental, decorations,
entertainment,
speakers,
and certain other expenses in addition to the cost of food,
beverages,
and other benefits provided.
(Eichmari Advice Letter,
No.
A—84—098,
Sutton Advice Letter,
No.
1-91-347.)
However,
the
Commission has also advised that when an
official attends an event but does not partake of an average
amount of food and beverage,
and does not stay and enjoy the
entertainment, it seems reasonable to attribute only the actual
value of the drink or other item received by the official.
(Sutton Advice Letter,
supra.)
You may wish to contact the sponsor of the event to obtain a
breakdown of the amounts attributed to you.
If you determine that
you received gifts totaling less than the amount attributed to you
by the sponsor,
you may calculate and disclose the fair market
value of those benefits which you actually received.
For example,
although you are not required to disclose any costs associated


Dana
W.
Reed
Page
3
with the tennis or golf events,
you must disclose the benefits
received at the dinner and dance,
including your pro rata share of
the sponsor’s payments for rental of the facilities,
decorations,
and entertainment.
If you have any questions concerning this letter,
please
contact me at
(916)
322_5662.2/
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
/7.
-
/
I,
C
C’
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
2/
Copies of Commission regulations and opinions are available
in many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials
and Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission
at a cost of bC per page.

Dana
W.
Reed

QUESTION: Are you required to disclose the pro—rata value of benefits
received at an event as determined by the sponsor of the event,
or
may you calculate and report the fair market value of what you
actually consumed at the event?

CONCLUSION: To determine the amount of benefit provided to you,
you must
include payments made by the sponsor for rental of the facilities,
entertainment,
decorations,
and other costs in addition to
payments for food,
beverages,
and other tangible benefits provided
to you.
However,
if you determine that the amount of benefit you
received is less than the amount attributed to you by the sponsor,
you may calculate the fair market value of the actual benefit you
received.,
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Dana

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission November 17, 1992 Dana W. Reed Reed & Davidson 3151 Airway Ave., Ste. M-l Costa Mesa, CA 92626 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-92-580 Dear Mr. Reed: You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 ! QTJESTION Are you required to disclose the pro—rata value of benefits received at an event as determined by the sponsor of the event, or may you calculate and report the fair market value of what you actually consumed at the event? CONCLUSION To determine the amount of benefit provided to you, you must include payments made by the sponsor for rental of the facilities, entertainment, decorations, and other costs in addition to payments for food, beverages, and other tangible benefits provided to you. However, if you determine that the amount of benefit you received is less than the amount attributed to you by the sponsor, you may calculate the fair market value of the actual benefit you received. Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • PD. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (9t6) 322-5660 Dana W. Reed Page 2 FACTS You are a member of the Orange County Transportation Authority Board of Directors and are required to file statements of economic interests (Form 730) pursuant to the Authority’s conflict of interest code. You attended a meeting of the board of directors of a nonprofit, tax-exempt business league held at a hotel in Orange County. The event included tennis and golf, a cocktail reception, dinner, and dancing. You did not participate in the golf or tennis activities. You attended the cocktail reception but did not drink, attended the dinner, and listened to some of the music. You have received notification from the sponsor that, according to their calculations, your pro rata share of the cost of the event was in excess of the $50 reporting threshold for gifts. ANALYSIS The Act requires public officials to file periodic reports disclosing their personal financial interests and income, including gifts. (Section 87200, et g.) “Gift” is defined in Section 82020 to mean: any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. The Commission has advised that for purposes of determining the amount of benefit provided to an official at a reception or other event like the one you attended, the sponsor must include payments for room rental, decorations, entertainment, speakers, and certain other expenses in addition to the cost of food, beverages, and other benefits provided. (Eichmari Advice Letter, No. A—84—098, Sutton Advice Letter, No. 1-91-347.)
